Name: latency-aware_spectral_stability_bounds
Title: Latency-Aware Inference via Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: Spectral properties of hidden states can serve as proxies for computational difficulty and stability, enabling dynamic resource allocation without training overhead.
Related Work: Existing methods for latency optimization rely on heuristic or static profiling, often failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Geometric Signal Separation (GSS) propose training-free frameworks based on activation geometry but do not explicitly link spectral properties to latency-aware optimization. Our proposal uniquely combines spectral diagnostics with PAC-Bayes bounds to enforce strict latency budgets.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for latency-aware inference that leverages spectral stability bounds derived from activation subspaces. By performing a single-pass PCA on hidden states, we identify spectral directions that govern computational difficulty and stability. Subsequent inference updates are projected onto these directions, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving optimization landscape geometry. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral properties to generalization stability under perturbation. Empirical evaluations on language models and mathematical reasoning tasks demonstrate that our method matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70%. Our approach provides a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient test-time scaling, bridging the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable inference.

Experiments.Description: Single-pass PCA on hidden states to identify spectral directions.
Projection of inference updates onto identified spectral directions.
Evaluation on language models and mathematical reasoning tasks.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Assumption that spectral stability alone is sufficient for latency optimization.
Limited generalization to domains beyond language models and mathematical reasoning.
Potential scalability issues in multi-GPU or distributed environments.
